coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots as a novel solution and paper strip-based dual sensor for the selective detection of Cr(VI) ions
coredata.dc:creator: John, Bony K.
John, Neenamol
Mathew, Sneha
Korah, Binila K.
Punnoose, Mamatha Susan
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report the development of a simple green methodology to fabricate N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with a high luminescence using R. graveolens leaves following the hydrothermal method. Optical, chemical, morphological, and electrochemical properties were thoroughly investigated employing a variety of analytical techniques. The fabricated N-CQDs have a spherical shape, with an average diameter of 4.5 nm. Excitation dependent emission behaviour, 18% QY and green fluorescence were all observed in the N-CQDs. They are particularly resistant to various impacts such as ionic strength, pH, continuous visible light irradiation, and storage time. Since Cr(VI) ions are highly carcinogenic and mutagenic, there is an urgent demand for sensitive, selective, and efficient sensors for monitoring and measuring the amount of Cr(VI) ion in water in the environment. Hence, we developed the prepared N-CQDs as a label-free fluorescence and electrochemical probe for detecting Cr(VI) ions selectively and sensitively in aqueous solutions. Relying on static quenching together with the inner filter effect, N-CQDs performed exceptionally well as fluorescence sensors for Cr(VI) ions. The N-CQDs exhibit a limit of detection of 300 and 5 nM in the photoluminescence and differential pulse voltammetry analysis, respectively. The detection of Cr(VI) ions in real water samples using the developed sensor through both methods presented high precision without any interference. In addition, we also developed paper sensing strips for field application by depositing N-CQD on filter paper, which has been satisfactorily evaluated for sensing Cr(VI) in solid-state and solution mediums.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots as a novel solution and paper strip-based dual sensor for the selective detection of Cr(VI) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills P. O, Kottayam 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills P. O, Kottayam- 686 560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report the development of a simple green methodology to fabricate N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with a high luminescence using R. graveolens leaves following the hydrothermal method. Optical, chemical, morphological, and electrochemical properties were thoroughly investigated employing a variety of analytical techniques. The fabricated N-CQDs have a spherical shape, with an average diameter of 4.5 nm. Excitation dependent emission behaviour, 18% QY and green fluorescence were all observed in the N-CQDs. They are particularly resistant to various impacts such as ionic strength, pH, continuous visible light irradiation, and storage time. Since Cr(VI) ions are highly carcinogenic and mutagenic, there is an urgent demand for sensitive, selective, and efficient sensors for monitoring and measuring the amount of Cr(VI) ion in water in the environment. Hence, we developed the prepared N-CQDs as a label-free fluorescence and electrochemical probe for detecting Cr(VI) ions selectively and sensitively in aqueous solutions. Relying on static quenching together with the inner filter effect, N-CQDs performed exceptionally well as fluorescence sensors for Cr(VI) ions. The N-CQDs exhibit a limit of detection of 300 and 5 nM in the photoluminescence and differential pulse voltammetry analysis, respectively. The detection of Cr(VI) ions in real water samples using the developed sensor through both methods presented high precision without any interference. In addition, we also developed paper sensing strips for field application by depositing N-CQD on filter paper, which has been satisfactorily evaluated for sensing Cr(VI) in solid-state and solution mediums.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are zero-dimensional materials with a particle size of less than 10 nm [1]. Besides the advantages such as intense fluorescence, easily tunable emission wavelength, excellent photostability and bio-imaging ability, most of the currently available QDs are costly, hydrophobic, and toxic due to the presence of some heavy metal elements like cadmium, mercury, lead etc. [2,3]. The significant efforts which were put forward by the scientific community for a more efficient, chemically inert, low cost, biocompatible and highly photoluminescent material finally led to the discovery of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). Despite being a new member of the carbon family, CQDs have garnered fascinating interest in the research field with fullerenes, graphene and carbon nanotubes. These materials have extended polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons inside them and were first identified as an impurity while purifying the single-walled nanotubes by Xu et al. in 2004 [4]. They exhibited several promising properties such as stable and tunable photoluminescence emission, varying band-gap, easy surface functionalisation and modification, facile and simple synthetic routes, semiconducting properties, minimal toxicity, excellent aqueous solubility, inexpensiveness, and high biocompatibility [5–9].

Several methods for synthesising CQDs have been reported over the last one and a half decades, including arc discharge, thermal cracking of organic compounds, photoablation, electrochemical oxidation, microwave mediated synthesis, ultrasonication and pyrolysis [10–13]. The resultant CQDs exhibit several interesting applications in optoelectronics [14], photovoltaic devices [15] antibacterial studies [16], treating inflammation [17], photodynamic therapy [18], sensors [19], photocatalyst [20], drug delivery [21], bio-imaging [22] and energy research [23]. In the present days, synthesis of CQDs via hydrothermal method using different environmentally friendly, economical, and readily available precursors such as lemon juice [24], orange use [25], cucumber peel [26], coal [27], honey [28], sweet potatoes [29] and garlic [30] have got significant attraction since it effectively eliminates the use and generation of costly and hazardous materials. These nanosensors are successful in detecting toxic metal ions, drugs, pesticides, amino acids, and other hazardous substances in the aqueous medium.

Recently, immense attention has been given to CQD based photoluminescent (PL) and electrochemical sensors for the identification of toxic metal cations such as Hg(II) [31], Pb(II) [32], Cu(II), Fe(II) [33] Cr(III) [34], Cr(VI) [35] ions etc. Out of these, Cr(VI) claims special attention since it is one of the widely accepted alloying elements and poses significant uses in industrial and other anthropogenic processes [36]. The Cr(VI) ions are extremely harmful to human health because of their greater mobility, high carcinogenicity, and mutagenesis characteristics [37]. The dangerous Cr(VI) can enter the body of a living system in various ways, including contaminated drinking water, food, and air. The World Health Organization (WHO) has set a maximum of 0.05 mg/L (approximately 1 μM) Cr(VI) ion concentration in potable water; therefore, it is essential to detect, monetise, and control the Cr(VI) level in the environment to reduce its harmful effects and lower the risk factors. In recent years, various analytical strategies have been successfully developed for this purpose, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [38], chromatography [39], plasma-optical emission spectroscopy [40], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [41] etc. However, the majority of these approaches involve costly equipment and time-consuming preparation steps, limiting their potential applicability. To overcome these challenges, both PL and electrochemical methods, which are low cost and exhibit high sensitivity, are widely used to detect water contaminants [42].

Inspired by the factors mentioned above, we introduce a simple, economical, greener and one-step approach for fabricating water-soluble N-CQDs from the Ruta graveolens (R. graveolens) leaves extract through a hydrothermal reaction and investigate their sensitivity and specificity for Cr(VI) ions detection using photoluminescence (PL) and electrochemical analysis. To the best of our investigation, no work has been reported for synthesising N-CQDs using the R. graveolens plant for the rapid fluorescent and electrochemical detection of Cr(VI) ions. R. graveolens is a herbal and ornamental plant that belongs to the family Rutaceae and is widely used in the medicinal field due to its excellent healing abilities and insect repellent activity. The synthesised N-CQD exhibited higher water dispersibility, excellent stability and bright green luminescence. PL analysis displayed an excellent linear response to Cr(VI) ions with a lower detection limit (LOD) of 300 nM over the 0 to 15 μM concentration range. The electrochemical analysis identified an excellent LOD of 5 nM with a linear response ranging between 20 and 70 nM. N-CQD coated paper sensing strips have been introduced for the real-time sensing of the Cr(VI) ions. We have also evaluated the practical viability of this technique for detecting traces of Cr(VI) ions in real sample experiments.

R.graveolens leaves were collected from the home garden. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS), quinine sulfate (99.0%), Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2.H2O, FeSO4.7H2O, HgCl2, K2Cr2O7, MnCl2.4H2O, CrCl3, CoCl2.6H2O, ZnSO4.7H2O, Fe(NO3)3.9H2O, CaCl2.6H2O, NiCl2.6H2O, CuSO4.5H2O, KCl and NaCl were obtained from Merck, India. All the analysis was carried out using distilled water.

Optical analyses were carried out in Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus UV–visible (UV–vis.) spectrometer in the 200–800 nm regions. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was collected using the Perkin-Elmer spectrometer between 400 and 4000 cm−1 range. A Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm was used to collect photoluminescence (PL) spectra. X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies were performed on a Bruker AXS D8 Advance Xray Diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation at 1.5406 Å wavelength. The morphological studies were investigated with the aid of transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images obtained from a JEOL-JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope. The Horiba SZ-100 scientific nanoparticle analyser was used to perform particle size analysis and zeta potential measurements. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigation was executed on a Physical Electronics instrument. Electrochemical experiments were performed on the Biologic SP-300 electrochemical workstation. The working, reference and counter electrodes were made of N-CQDs/GCE, Ag/AgCl and Pt wires, respectively.

In a standard procedure, fresh leaves of R. graveolens were initially appropriately cleaned with tap water and then with distilled water. Further, the leaves were crushed into a paste using a home-use mixer grinder without the addition of water. The next step was to dissolve 10 g of paste in 100 mL of distilled water. The suspension was placed into a Teflon-lined autoclave with a capacity of 250 mL and maintained in the oven for 5 h at 180 °C. The autoclave was left to cool naturally to room temperature when the reaction was completed, and the residues were removed through filtration. The obtained brown coloured supernatant was centrifuged for 15 min at 15000/rpm to eliminate the larger molecules. A dialysis membrane (1 K MWCO) was used to purify the fabricated N-CQDs in distilled water for 2–3 days. Finally, a freeze drier was used to dry the solution, and the final concentration was adjusted to 0.25 mg/mL by re-dispersing in water. The synthetic procedure is presented in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorbance, fluorescence behaviour and quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption maxima of the N-CQD in aqueous dispersion were observed at 282 and 221 nm. Excitation of the N-CQD solution at 380 nm resulted in a prominent emission band at 415 nm. The comparative method was used to determine the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) using quinine sulphate (0.1 M H2SO4 Ф = 54%) as the reference [43]. More details regarding the QY calculation are given in the supplementary material section (Fig. S1).

Fluorescence analyses were conducted in the aqueous medium to explore the selectivity of synthesised N-CQDs towards Cr(VI) ions compared to other metal ions. Some common metal ions namely Na(I), K(I), Mn(II), Zn(II), Fe(II), Ca(II), Hg(II), Ni(II), Cd(II), Cr(II), Pb(II), Cr(VI), Cu(II), Co(II), and Fe(III) ions were used for the study. All the analyses were performed at pH 7 using PBS at normal temperature. During the experiment, 200 μL solutions of metal salts having 20 μM concentration were added to a mixture containing 100 μL of 0.25 mg/mL N-CQDs solution and 2.5 mL of PBS. The samples were thoroughly shaken for 4 min. Fluorescence analysis of the mixture under room temperature revealed the high selectivity of the N-CQDs towards Cr(VI), which causes a quenching in PL intensity. To investigate the sensitivity of the sensor for Cr(VI) ions, the solution of Cr(VI) ions having known concentrations was mixed with the N-CQD solution at pH 7, with the final concentration ranging from 0 to 30 μM. The mixture solution was allowed to interact for 4 min, and the PL spectra were recorded under similar experimental conditions.

A similar strategy was executed to examine the interference from other metal ions. For that, about 50 μM of individual metal ions and 20 μM Cr(VI) were added to the N-CQD-buffer mixture, and PL spectra of the solutions were collected. A bar diagram was obtained by plotting F/Fo against mixtures of various metal ions. In triplicate, both the selectivity and sensitivity of the system were investigated.

The working electrode was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE), the counter electrode was Pt wire, and the reference electrode was Ag/AgCl electrode. Initially, GCE was polished with a 0.05 μM alumina slurry, and N-CQD modified GCE (N-CQD/GCE) was developed by drop-casting about 0.25 mg/mL of the sample over the GCE surface and drying it at room temperature. This N-CQD/GCE was used for all further electrochemical experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CQD coated paper strips for Cr(VI) ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: The cellulose filter paper was sliced into small rectangular sheets and dipped in the aqueous N-CQD solution. The paper sheets were removed from the solution after 4 min, and allowed to dry at room temperature. The aqueous metal ion solutions were drop casted onto the N-CQD coated paper strip and kept for drying at room temperature. The solid-state detection of metal ions was carried out by placing a little of metal salt at one end of the N-CQD coated filter paper and was withdrawn after 4 min. The difference in fluorescence of the filter paper strip was examined using a UV lamp at 360 nm.

Analytical, spectroscopic, and microscopic studies were employed to investigate the morphology, microstructure, and size distribution of the N-CQDs. The TEM analysis of N-CQD, given in Fig. 1a, shows that the nanoparticles are well separated and fairly round-shaped without apparent aggregation. The lattice fringes were prominent in the HR-TEM images shown in Fig. 1b with 0.21 nm lattice spacing, close to the interspacing of the (002) plane of graphitic structure [44]. The lattice fringes were noticed since there were few crystalline N-CQDs in the system. The broad circular ring in the selected area of electron diffraction (SAED) micrograph pattern in Fig. 1c indicates the amorphous structure of the prepared N-CQDs. The size distribution histogram of N-CQDs shown in Fig. 1d confirmed that size varies from 2 to 8 nm, with a 4.5 nm average size. DLS experiments was also executed to examine the size apportionment of the developed N-CQDs (Fig. S2). The average diameter of the N-CQDs has a distribution between 1 and 10 nm, which is in best congruent with the results received in TEM analysis.

The existence of elements nitrogen (N), carbon (C), and oxygen (O) were confirmed by the EDX analysis with an elemental composition of about C 51.81 wt%, O 37.06 wt%, and N 11.13 wt% (Fig. 2a). The FT-IR studies were followed to understand the various functional groups and linkages in the synthesised sensor. As shown in Fig. 2b, N-CQD exhibited a broad absorption band in the 3350–3250 cm−1 range as a result of stretching vibrations from the NH bond of amine groups and the OH bond in phenolic groups. These bands indicate that the principal passivating groups on the N-CQD surface are hydroxyl and amine groups. The vibration band at 2930 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CH bond. A strong band observed at 1767 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibrations of CO group. Another band at 1578 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of CC and the band at 1386 cm−1 is assigned to NH bending vibrations. The typical bending band of the amine CN bond is observed at 1077 cm-1, and the band at 1043 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CO bond bending vibrations [45]. The functional groups such as hydroxyl, and amino groups present on the surface of N-CQDs are responsible for their excellent hydrophilicity, stability, and PL emission ability.

The XRD analysis shown in Fig. 2c exhibited a broad and noisy peak at 2θ = 24.12°, further confirms the amorphous surface structure of the N-CQDs. The peak was originated from the (002) Bragg reflection of graphitic lattice plane [46,47]. The presence of surface hydroxyl and amine functional groups confirmed by FT-IR analysis is responsible for the amorphous nature [48]. The Raman spectrum shown in Fig. 2d shows two broad Raman peaks at 1360 and 1582 cm−1 associated with the sp3 hybridised disordered D and sp2 hybridised graphitic G bands, respectively [49]. The vibration of carbon atoms containing dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite structure is responsible for the D band. The G band originates from the E2g vibrations of sp2 hybridised graphitic carbon atoms, in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [50]. The symbols ID and IG denote peak intensities of the D and G bands, respectively. In the Raman spectra, G band exhibited higher intensity than that of the D band is due to the highly ordered carbon arrangements in the synthesised N-CQDs. The ID/IG intensity ratio calculated was 0.53, which is less than one, indicating that highly graphitised carbon atoms are present in the synthesised N-CQDs [51]. Therefore, the Raman result suggests that fabricated N-CQD is mainly composed of sp2 hybrid carbons with some defected sp3 hybrid carbons with a graphitic structure.

The surface states and chemical composition were investigated using XPS analysis. The N-CQDs (Fig. 3a) exhibit three distinct signals at 285.5, 402 and 533 eV, in the survey spectrum related to C1s, N1s and O1s peaks. The analysis revealed the presence of 69.1% C, 25.4% O and 5.5% N in the developed system. Since the carbons present in the system are exposed to four different chemical environments, the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) produced four different peaks at 288.75, 286.63, 285.11, and 284.42, eV, corresponding to the C=O/C=N, CO, CN, and C=C/C-C species, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) exhibits three peaks at 534.04, 532.89, and 531.60 eV, corresponding to C–OH/C–O–C, CO, and CO species, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) was resolved into two distinct peaks, each at 401.15, and 399.20 eV associated with NH, and CN bonds, respectively [52,53,54].

UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence analysis, quantum yield (QY) calculations, and emission lifetime assessments were used to examine the optical characteristics of N-CQDs. The UV–vis spectrum of N-CQDs depicted in Fig. 4a shows two distinct peaks at 221 and 282 nm. The strong absorption band at 282 nm can be assigned to the n–π* transitions of the CO bonds, and a second peak around 221 nm is ascribed to π-π* transition of the conjugated CC bonds. As displayed in the inset of Fig. 4a, the N-CQD aqueous solution appeared light brown under visible light. Fig. 4b shows the fluorescence emission peak obtained at 458 nm when exciting the fluorophore at 380 nm associated with a green fluorescence as given in the inset of Fig. 4b. The excitation dependent emission character of fabricated N-CQDs was investigated by collecting the PL spectra at various excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 440 nm with a rise of 10 nm and is given in Fig. S3. The PL spectra initially showed an increase in intensity with the excitation wavelength, reaching a highest value at 458 nm at an excitation of 380 nm and then decreasing, confirming the excitation dependent emission property. Moreover, increasing the excitation wavelength resulted in a clear red shift of the emission peak from 450 nm to 510 nm, followed by a change in PL intensity. This excitation-dependent emission intensity, same as that of other luminescent carbon nanoparticles, results from the different surface states and sizes of N-CQDs [55]. This property allows them to function as a fluorescent probe for a variety of sensing applications. Following the established procedure and taking quinine sulphate (54%) as a reference, the fluorescence QY of the N-CQD solution was determined to be 18% at 380 nm excitation [43].

The fluorescence stability of N-CQDs has been analysed by recording PL intensities of the solutions at various experimental conditions such as ionic strength, store time, photo irradiation and pH. The F and Fo in the plot reflect the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQD solution at a certain experimental condition and that of the untreated N-CQD solution, respectively. The fluorescence intensity and peak position did not change significantly with the KCl concentration range of 0 to 2 M, as depicted in Fig. S4a. This ability enabled the N-CQDs to be used in highly concentrated salt environments without aggregation with a stable PL emission. In their discrete state, the PL emission intensity of fabricated N-CQDs is stable only in the pH range of 4 to 8, after which it becomes unstable (Fig. S4b). It is probably due to the interaction between the surface-bound functional groups with H+ and OH− ions at lower and higher pH levels, which resulted in carbon dot aggregation, identical to the trends observed in previously reported studies [56–58]. The PL emission intensity was almost unaffected during continuous visible light irradiation for 2 h, as demonstrated in Fig. S4c. The homogeneous N-CQD solution does not undergo precipitation or photobleaching during the analysis, which confirms the excellent photostability of the system. Furthermore, only a minor change in PL intensity was observed for a sample kept at room temperature for three months compared with those of freshly prepared N-CQDs (Fig. S4d). These observations demonstrate the improved stability of N-CQD, which allows them to be a suitable PL probe for valuable commercial purposes and practical applications.

The influence of pH on the fluorescence response to Cr(VI) ions was investigated to enhance the performance of the sensor. The emission spectra of a set of N-CQD solutions having pH ranging from 3 to 12 were recorded upon adding 20 μM Cr(VI) ions. Fig. S5a shows that, although Cr(VI) ions exhibit different quenching effects at different pH values, the maximum quenching efficiency was obtained at pH 7. The optimal interaction time for quenching the fluorescence was investigated by adding 20 μM Cr(VI) ions to the N-CQD solution at pH 7. As evident from Fig. S5b, maximum interaction occurred within 4 min of mixing, so extending the interaction time was unnecessary. All subsequent analyses were carried out at pH 7 and 4 min interaction time from these results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigations on interaction with metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The surface of the N-CQD was found to be negatively charged (−23.9 mV), as evident from the zeta potential measurement (Fig. 5a). Thus, sensing applications arise from the interaction of the surface functional groups of N-CQDs with the positively charged metal ions. The sensing potency was determined by adding various metal ions to the N-CQD/buffer system with a total concentration of 20 μM as described in the experimental section, and corresponding PL emission spectra were recorded (Fig. 5b). The PL emission of N-CQDs remained almost unaffected at these concentrations of all metal ions except for the Cr(VI) ions. This significant quenching in emission intensity by the Cr(VI) ions at a very low concentration demonstrates high selectivity and specificity of N-CQDs for Cr(VI) ions over other metal ions, which is probably owing to the stronger interaction of Cr(VI) to the hydroxyl and amine groups on the N-CQD surface.

For a better understanding of the quenching phenomenon, fluorescence quenching behaviour was explored according to the Stern-Volmer equation,
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 + Ksv
Q
where F0 and F represents the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Cr(VI) ions respectively at emission wavelength 460 nm. [Q] is the fluorescence quencher concentration, and Ksv is the quenching constant. Fig. 5c represents the decrease in PL emission intensities on increasing the Cr6+ ion concentration from 0 to 30 μM. Fig. 5d demonstrates a plot of F0/F against Cr(VI) ions concentration. An excellent linear part for calculating the quenching constant is obtained in the 0–15 μM range with a linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997. The Ksv value calculated was 1.50 × 108 M−1 indicating that metal ions exhibit a moderately strong interaction with functional groups on the N-CQD surface. The LOD value calculated from the PL data was 300 nM according to Eq. (2).
(2)
LOD = 3S / m
where S is the standard deviation of the signals, and m is the slope of the linear fit. Fig. 5e displays the photograph of vials during UV irradiation, which acknowledges the very selective fluorescence quenching by Cr(VI) ions. According to the WHO, the permissible concentration of Cr(VI) ions in drinking water is less than 0.05 mg/L (approximately 1 μM) [59]. Hence the developed N-CQD based fluorescent sensor is favourable for determining the concentration of Cr(VI) ions in drinking water. A brief comparison of the results obtained in this system with those obtained in other recently reported systems (Table 1 ) shows that the results of this work are comparable to or better than many previously reported results.

Apart from sensitivity and selectivity, interference studies are another essential parameter to consider when evaluating sensor performance. For this purpose, we assessed the changes in PL intensity by adding 50 μM of different metal ions, including Ni(II), K(II), Fe(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Pb(II), Hg(II), Mn(II), Cu(II), Ca(II), Na(II), Cd(II), Fe(II) and Cr(III) to the N-CQD system containing 20 μM, Cr(VI) ions, using the procedure outlined in the experimental section. The bar diagrams shown in Fig. 6a suggest that Cr(VI) ions effectively quenched the intensity of N-CQD emission even in the interference of other metal ions. These findings demonstrated that the proposed system could serve as a unique fluorescent probe to detect the Cr(VI) ions in real water samples.

The FT-IR and XPS analysis show that the hydroxyl and amine functional groups are available on the N-CQDs surface. Because of the strong interaction of Cr(VI) ions with these functional groups, Cr(VI) ions aggregate around the N-CQDs, which results in the quenching of fluorescence [63]. The extent of quenching was found to increase with an increase in the concentration of Cr(VI) ions. There are two types of fluorescence quenching processes that are possible; static quenching and dynamic/collisional quenching. In the case of dynamic quenching, the fluorescence lifetime of the sensor changes when an analyte is added. Here, the effective collision occurred between the excited photoluminescent molecule and the quencher. However, in the case of static quenching, the fluorescence lifetime of the sensor remains constant in the presence and absence of the analyte. It involves a complex formation between the ground state photoluminescent molecule and the quencher [64]. Since molecular collisions are a must and necessary condition for both the quenching processes, the metal ion with the highest binding affinity for the functional groups on the N-CQD surface can produce selective fluorescence quenching compared to others (Fig. 6b). The obtained tri-exponential fitting indicates the availability of two or more emissive components in the N-CQDs. The average fluorescence lifetime of N-CQD was found to be 4.21 ns with χ2 value of 1.02 in the absence of Cr(VI) ions, whereas that after the addition of Cr(VI) ions became 4.05 ns with χ2 value 1.04. Since the lifetimes before and after adding Cr(VI) ions do not significantly change, the possibility of static quenching is confirmed between the sensor and the analyte.

There are three mechanisms possible for describing the fluorescence quenching phenomenon; fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE). Since Cr(VI) ions do not emit photoluminescence when exposed to UV light, the possibility of FRET can be ruled out. Furthermore, the lifetimes of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/Cr(VI) systems are nearly identical; there is no chance for a considerable electron-transfer process between Cr(VI)ions and N-CQDs, which rules out the possibility of PET. The IFE phenomenon usually occurs between the quencher and the fluorophore due to the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light by the quencher molecules. A highly efficient IFE necessitates sufficient spectral overlap of the absorption band of the quencher with the excitation and/or emission bands of the fluorophore. Hence, developing an appropriate quencher-fluorophore pair is essential in an IFE-based fluorescence sensing process [65]. The UV–vis spectra of N-CQD, Cr(VI) solution (20 μM) and N-CQD/Cr(VI) mixture (Fig. S6) showed a considerable change in absorption in the 600–300 nm range, indicating the occurrence of IFE mechanism in the sensing process. The Cr(VI) ions displayed three broad absorption peaks at 260, 350, and 450 nm. During the sensing process, the developed N-CQDs with PL excitation wavelength 380 nm and the emission wavelength of 460 nm exhibited a proper overlap with the absorption peaks of Cr(VI) ions. This permits the Cr(VI) ions to absorb the light emitted by the N-CQDs, owing to the quenching of PL intensity.

In addition, the positions of FT-IR bands in N-CQDs at 3270, 1582, and 1386 cm−1 corresponding to the –OH and –C=O and NH bond vibrations were shifted to 3172, 1498, and 1330 cm−1, respectively after the addition of Cr(VI) ions (Fig. S7). An additional band at 523 cm−1 further validate the coordination of Cr(VI) with the sensor. The findings support the excellent selectivity of N-CQDs for Cr(VI), which is related to the unique coordination interaction of Cr(VI) with different surface functional groups present in the N-CQDs [66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of N-CQD coated paper strips for sensing Cr(VI) ions
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQD coated paper strips were fabricated for simple and real-time sensing applications using the procedures outlined in the experimental section. The fabricated paper strips were immersed in different metal ions solutions for 5 min and allowed to dry at room temperature. The photographs of the N-CQD coated paper strips and those loaded with varying ions of metal under UV light exposure are presented in Fig. S8. The paper strips dipped in Cr(VI) ion solution exhibit significant colour changes, implying that they can be used to identify Cr(VI) ions in aqueous media. For the solid-state detection of Cr(VI) ion, a little of K2Cr2O7 was placed on the N-CQD coated filter paper, and the solid was removed after 5 min. A noticeable colour change was observed on the paper strips under UV light, where the metal salts were initially placed (Fig. S9). This observation demonstrates the exceptional capacity of the sensor to identify the Cr(VI) ions even in the solid-state.

The analytical performance of the synthesised sensor was further investigated by electrochemical analysis. The N-CQD modified glassy carbon electrode (N-CQD/GCE) was applied to develop an efficient and highly sensitive electrochemical sensor to detect Cr(VI) ions. To investigate the electrochemical sensing performance of the fabricated sensor, cyclic voltammetric (CV) experiments were done within a potential range from −0.1 to 0.6 V in PBS, having 50 nM of different metal ion solutions with a scan rate of 50 mV/s (Fig. S10). Typical CV sweep curves of bare GCE (black line), modified N-CQD/GCE in the absence (red line), and presence (blue line) of Cr(VI) ions is depicted in Fig. 7a. When no Cr(VI) ion was present and only the GCE was employed, no significant peak was noticed in the CV curve. However, a well-defined oxidation peak current at 0.0037 A corresponds to 0.385 V and a reduction peak at −0.0034 A corresponding to 0.121 V was observed when 50 nM Cr(VI) ion was added to the system. These results suggest that the N-CQD is an efficient electron mediator in this process. The reduction reaction occurred on the N-CQD/GCE surface, with electrons being transferred from Cr(VI) to Cr(III) during the reduction process and from Cr(III) to Cr(VI) during the oxidation process.

pH studies were performed in 50 nM solutions of Cr(VI) ions in PBS with pH ranges ranging from 3 to 8. As depicted in Fig. 7b, the highest peak current was observed at pH 4; thus, all further analysis was done at this pH. To examine the influence of the scanning rate on the redox reaction, CV analysis was carried out at different scan rates. The obtained voltammograms illustrated in Fig. 7c demonstrate that the peak current of Cr(VI) ions increases as the scan rate increases. Since peak positions do not change much with the scan rate, the process is assumed to be reversible. Fig. 7d shows a plot of current versus the scan rate, which exhibits a linear relationship suggesting that diffusion controls the electrocatalytic process [67].

Further, DPV analysis was carried out to observe peak intensity changes with the Cr(VI) ion concentration. The analysis was performed at a fixed scan rate of 50 mV/s with Cr(VI) ion concentrations ranging from 20 to 70 nM in PBS at pH 4. Fig. 7e clearly shows that concentration has a positive influence on the current intensity. Since the current value increases with Cr(VI) ion concentration, a diffusion-controlled process was responsible for the sensing. The validity of this analysis could be proven by plotting the calibration curve of the catalytic current versus Cr(VI) ions concentration, as seen in Fig. 7f. This observation revealed that the linearity increases with increasing the Cr(VI) ions concentrations. This novel electrochemical sensor exhibited a very low LOD of 5 nM and a linear range between 20 and 70 nm with excellent reproducibility. Table 2 shows the potential advantages of the developed sensor by comparing.
with the previously reported electrochemical sensors.

Further, the selective detection ability of the fabricated N-CQD/GCE towards Cr(VI) was evaluated using the chronoamperometric technique between 0 and 200 s. The amperometric response of the modified electrode towards the addition of Cr(VI) ions (50 nm) in the interference of various other interfering analytes such as Fe(II), Cr(III), Mn(II), Hg(II), Pb(II), Zn(II), Fe(III), Na(II), Cu(II), and K(I) with a concentration five times that of the Cr(VI) ions is depicted in Fig. 8a. A considerable change in current was seen with the addition of Cr(VI) ions only, which suggests the selective sensing ability of the developed sensor.

The reproducibility of the sensor was evaluated through CV measurements by keeping the N-CQD/GCE in a desiccator for one month. The graph was recorded within the potential range of −0.10 to 0.60 V using the freshly modified electrode, and the same electrode after one month did not exhibit any significant difference in the peak current intensities. Because of the high reproducibility of the fabricated sensor, only a 1.60% decrease in peak current intensity was observed (Fig. 8b). The CV technique was used to test the stability of the developed sensor in a 50 nM solution of Cr(VI) ions in PBS for seventy continuous cycles. The peak current occurrence varied only slightly between the first and last cycles, indicating the excellent stability of the fabricated sensor (Fig. 8c).

The performance of the sensor was further assessed with the aid of potentio electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (PEIS). The impedance spectra obtained in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- and 0.1 M KCl solutions at frequencies ranging from 5 Hz to 150 MHz is shown in Fig. 8d. The spectrum produces a semicircle which indicates the resistance of the sensor. The modified N-CQD/GCE sensor offers less resistance in comparison with the bare electrode resulting in a faster electron transfer response rate, validating the sensor's outstanding activity.

The negative peak current was presumably caused by the reduction of Cr(VI) ions to Cr(III) ions, whereas the positive current was caused by the oxidation of Cr(III) ions to Cr(VI) ions, as previously discussed. Electrochemical detection involves the reduction of Cr(VI) ions on the surface of the N-CQD/GCE electrode through an electron capture reaction. The reduction becomes faster at pH 4, consistent with previously reported works [71,72]. The plausible electron transfer mechanism for the Cr(VI) ion at N-CQDs/GCE is shown in Scheme 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis using PL and DPV
ce:section.ce:para:
The N-CQD-based detection technique for the Cr(VI) ions was further examined in real samples using PL and electrochemical methods to verify the feasibility, selectivity, and quantitative detection ability of the proposed sensor. For this, tap water and river water samples were collected. The samples collected were initially filtered using a 0.20 μm filter and centrifuged up to 10,000 rpm for 20 min. Different amounts of Cr(VI) ions having known concentrations were introduced into the real samples, and the recovery rate results were measured by PL and DPV analysis. The obtained results are tabulated in Tables 3 and 4
. The observed recovery is ranged from 98 to 101% in both methods. The calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) for the three replicated analyses is less than 2% in PL and 0.28 in DPV analysis. These values demonstrate the potential applications of the PL and DPV based sensing method for quick and accurate detection of Cr(VI) ions in the aqueous medium.

For the very first time, we have successfully established a simple and environmentally friendly approach for synthesising N-CQDs (QY = 18%) from R. graveolens leaves using a hydrothermal process. They exhibit a spherical shape and an average particle size of about 4.5 nm. The N-CQDs were stable under ambient conditions and served as a good fluorescence and electrochemical sensor for Cr(VI) ions. As a PL sensor, they are very selective, and the extent of quenching is determined by the extent of Cr(VI) ion coordination with the amine and hydroxyl groups on the N-CQD surface. The sensing process involves a static quenching associated IFE mechanism. This method exhibits an excellent linear range from 0 to 15 μM with a LOD of 300 nM. Further, we fabricated N-CQD modified GCE (N-CQD/GCE) for the electrochemical sensing of Cr(VI) ions with excellent stability and reproducibility. The N-CQD/GCE exhibits a LOD of 5 nM with a 20 to 70 nM linear range. The developed probe was successfully used to detect Cr(VI) ions in real sample analysis using PL and DPV methods, with high precision and no interference, and provided excellent detection efficiency. Finally, N-CQD coated paper strips were developed and applied to detect Cr (VI) ions in aqueous and solid environments for simple and real-time applications.
